data.name: InterventionGuidance_Title
data.value: Intervention philosophy

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Subtitle
data.value: No intervention without indication. Do no harm. A movement pattern only becomes a problem when it is a problem in its specific context.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactIntro
data.value: Running style is a self-optimizing system. We do not correct the appearance of movement; we check whether a dynamic pattern is actually linked to symptoms, load, or missing control.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TalkTitle
data.value: How we talk about running style

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ReviewTitle
data.value: When we look more closely

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactTalk_1
data.value: A running-style note is not a judgment of right or wrong.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactTalk_2
data.value: We look together at whether a pattern fits symptoms, load, or fatigue.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactTalk_3
data.value: The goal is understanding and options, not an obligation to correct.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactReview_1
data.value: A visible deviation alone is not yet a problem.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactReview_2
data.value: It becomes more relevant when the evidence is good and the pattern is dynamically visible.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_CompactReview_3
data.value: With weaker evidence, symptoms and plausible links matter most.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SystemUnderstandingTitle
data.value: From form correction to system understanding

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SystemUnderstandingText
data.value: In the past, the drill was often: everyone needs a perfect-looking form. Today, the better stance is: bodies differ, running style is individual, and a movement pattern is not automatically wrong just because it differs from an ideal image. The body is a self-optimizing system. Many runners choose cadence, stride length, and movement strategy very close to an economical solution for them, especially as experience grows

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FunctioningSystemAlert
data.value: If an athlete has been symptom-free for years with a certain running style, specifically symptom-free with regard to that exact movement pattern, this is initially a sign of a well-functioning dynamic system. Even a well-intentioned intervention can shift that system in a negative direction.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsTitle
data.value: Interventions have side effects

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsIntro
data.value: Technique changes rarely just reduce load; they shift it. Less knee load can mean more Achilles tendon, calf, or foot load. More stability can mean more stiffness. A different foot strike can overload new structures

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsIndicationIntro
data.value: Interventions therefore need an indication. This can be:

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsIndication_1
data.value: Moderate to good evidence showing a positive cost-benefit relationship for future injuries.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsIndication_2
data.value: Low to moderate evidence together with symptoms associated with the intervention.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SideEffectsConclusion
data.value: For most common criteria of good running form, these requirements are not met.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ConsentPowerDynamicsTitle
data.value: Consent and power dynamics

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ConsentPowerDynamicsText
data.value: Of course, it can also make sense to modify individual running-style parameters for performance reasons. At the same time, every intervention has a price: it does not simply remove load, but shifts it within the system and can create new side effects. Whether a possible performance gain justifies accepting those side effects cannot be dictated by shouting from the sideline, group pressure, or a quick correction. This trade-off belongs in long-term, individual support and requires the athlete's informed consent. Even when a coach notices or dislikes an aspect of the running movement, they need to remember that nobody sees the whole system from the outside. Training history, symptoms, goals, fears, prior experiences, and current adaptability all belong to the decision. Because recommendations from a coaching role always carry weight, and because it can be difficult for athletes to decline them, we approach running-style interventions with particular care: not as instructions, but as shared decisions on equal footing.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ControlIllusionTitle
data.value: Avoiding the illusion of control

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ControlIllusionText
data.value: We believe that many old-school coaches quite often fall into an illusion of control. They give a correction, the athlete improves over weeks, but possibly not because of the correction. Training, tissue, coordination, and self-organization may have been adapting anyway. The system optimizes itself; the coach thinks: I fixed the form. Conversely, a correction can shift load and trigger symptoms. Then the coach says: see, that happened because you always run like this or that. In both cases, causality is claimed without being clearly established. That is exactly why we need indication, evidence, and context

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Talk_1
data.value: A note about running style is not a judgment of whether you run right or wrong.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Talk_2
data.value: Your own perception matters: what do you feel, when does it occur, what changes it?

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Talk_3
data.value: We always view running style in the context of symptoms, training, shoes, pace, volume, and fatigue.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Talk_4
data.value: You receive an interpretation and possible options, not an obligation to correct your form.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_RelevanceTitle
data.value: When a pattern becomes relevant

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Relevance_1
data.value: A visible deviation alone is not yet a problem. Many running styles work very well.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Relevance_2
data.value: A pattern becomes more important when evidence is good and it is dynamically visible while running.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Relevance_3
data.value: With moderate or weak evidence, the key question is whether matching symptoms, fatigue, or loss of control are also present.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_Relevance_4
data.value: Without symptoms or a plausible load link, an unusual pattern usually remains individual variation.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_EvidenceTitle
data.value: Evidence grades and benefit for injury prevention

data.name: InterventionGuidance_EvidenceSubtitle
data.value: Benefit scale: Very high, High, Medium, Low, Very low, None. It estimates how strongly an intervention on the feature can contribute to injury prevention when a concrete indication is present.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TableFeature
data.value: Feature

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TableEvidence
data.value: Evidence grade

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TableScore
data.value: Score

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TableBenefit
data.value: Benefit for injury prevention

data.name: InterventionGuidance_TableRelevantWhen
data.value: When relevant?

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitVeryHigh
data.value: Very high

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitHigh
data.value: High

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitMedium
data.value: Medium

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitLow
data.value: Low

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitVeryLow
data.value: Very low

data.name: InterventionGuidance_BenefitNone
data.value: None

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureHipDrop
data.value: Hip drop opposite the stance leg

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureLegInternalRotation
data.value: Internal rotation of the leg axis

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureOverstride
data.value: Overstride

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureForwardLean
data.value: Forward trunk lean

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureHipExtension
data.value: Hip extension

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureLordosis
data.value: Excessive lumbar arch

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureTrunkRotation
data.value: Trunk rotation

data.name: InterventionGuidance_FeatureArmAngle
data.value: Arm angle

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdHipDrop
data.value: With dynamic expression, asymmetry, knee/ITB/pelvic symptoms.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdLegInternalRotation
data.value: With dynamic valgus, knee symptoms, ITB symptoms, or loss of control.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdOverstride
data.value: With marked expression, braking impulse, shin/knee symptoms, or load peaks.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdForwardLean
data.value: Context-dependent: fatigue, hip/back symptoms, or clear compensation.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdHipExtension
data.value: Relevant with missing hip extension and compensatory pelvis/lumbar movement.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdLordosis
data.value: Do not judge statically; only with dynamic overextension and symptoms.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdTrunkRotation
data.value: Only with clear asymmetry, symptoms, or coordination problem.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_ThresholdArmAngle
data.value: Usually not an injury problem; more often rhythm, economy, or tension.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourcesTitle
data.value: Sources

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceCeyssens
data.value: Ceyssens et al. Biomechanical risk factors associated with running-related injuries: a systematic review. Sports Med. 2019.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceWillwacher
data.value: Willwacher et al. Running-related biomechanical risk factors for overuse injuries in distance runners. Sports Med. 2022.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceAndersonStepRate
data.value: Anderson et al. What is the effect of changing running step rate on injury, performance and biomechanics? Sports Med Open. 2022.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceMoore
data.value: Moore. Is there an economical running technique? Sports Med. 2016.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceNigg
data.value: Nigg et al. Running shoes and running injuries: preferred movement path and comfort filter. Br J Sports Med. 2015.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceGaitRetraining
data.value: Clinical application of gait retraining in the injured runner. PMC.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceRidge
data.value: Ridge et al. Foot bone marrow edema after a 10-week transition to minimalist running shoes. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2013.

data.name: InterventionGuidance_SourceChristopher
data.value: Christopher et al. Do strength, flexibility, ROM and alignment predict lower extremity injury in runners? Arch Physiother. 2019.
